topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom glittered under a thousand suspended lights, a dazzling, suffocating spectacle. I watched from across the room, nursing a lukewarm champagne, my gaze locked onto the familiar silhouette near the cake table. There she was, laughing, radiant in a dress that seemed specifically designed to draw attention. A slow, cold tightening began in my chest, a familiar, unpleasant clenching. I took a deliberate, slow sip, letting the silence stretch, savoring the moment before I moved. When our eyes finally met across the crowded floor, I didn't offer a smile. Instead, I offered a slight, almost imperceptible tilt of my head—a gesture that held more judgment than warmth. I made my way over, my steps measured and deliberate, ensuring every glance I cast was calibrated to sting. "Fancy seeing you here," I murmured, my voice smooth as polished stone, the words carrying just enough edge to suggest I hadn't forgotten the past, and certainly hadn't forgotten what was lost.

[story 2]

The air in the reception hall was thick with forced joy and expensive perfume. I’d rehearsed this moment in my head a hundred times, mapping out the precise trajectory of my approach. Seeing him—my former partner—standing beside a new acquaintance made my jaw tighten. A distinct heat rose in my neck, a prickly sensation that fueled my resolve. I moved through the throng, my focus narrowing until only he remained in my sightline. When I reached him, I didn't stop to exchange pleasantries. I leaned in close enough that I could smell the cologne, and my tone dropped to a low, almost conspiratorial register. "It's quite the coincidence, isn't it?" I asked, my eyes sweeping over him with an intensity that felt like an interrogation. I watched the flicker of surprise cross his face, a small victory I immediately registered as mine. I didn't wait for a reply; I simply straightened up, gave a curt nod, and turned my attention back to the periphery, leaving him with the lingering impression of my deliberate disdain.

[story 3]

I stood near the edge of the patio, pretending to examine a floral arrangement, but every nerve ending was screaming. The music was too loud, the chatter too saccharine. Then, there he was, bathed in the warm glow of the string lights, looking utterly happy. A sudden, sharp impatience seized me. I felt a distinct hardening in my stomach, a coiled tension ready to snap. I decided to interrupt the tableau. I walked directly toward him, ignoring the curious glances from the nearby tables. When I stopped just a few feet away, I let my expression settle into something cool and utterly unreadable. "Well, look what the tide dragged in," I said, my voice carrying a deliberate, mocking lightness. I let the silence hang between us, heavy and expectant. I watched his composure waver, a tiny crack appearing in his façade, and I allowed myself a small, internal satisfaction at the subtle shift in his demeanor.
